title: Binary (Cash-or-Nothing) Option

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: A cash-or-nothing binary call pays $1 at maturity if S_T > K, else $0. Find its price under BSM.
rules: Risk-neutral pricing: c_B = exp(-rT) * E^Q[1{S_T > K}] = exp(-rT) * Q(S_T > K).
Under GBM with risk-neutral drift r and vol sigma: Q(S_T > K) = N(d_2).
setup.question: Price the binary call and discuss hedging.

learningObjectives: Recognize that the discounted RN probability gives the binary price.
Identify N(d_2) as the RN probability that S_T > K.
Discuss hedging difficulty near expiry when S ~ K (delta becomes singular).

stages.label: Risk-neutral pricing
stages.prompt: Binary call payoff at T: 1 if S_T > K, 0 else. The risk-neutral price is...?

options.text: c_B = exp(-rT) * Q(S_T > K) = exp(-rT) * N(d_2).

stages.explanation: Under Q, S_T = S * exp((r - sigma^2/2)T + sigma * sqrt(T) * Z). The probability S_T > K reduces to a Gaussian tail: Q(S_T > K) = N(d_2). Discounted, the price is exp(-rT) * N(d_2).

stages.label: Hedging near expiry
stages.prompt: Delta of the binary call is dc_B/dS = exp(-rT) * N'(d_2) / (S * sigma * sqrt(T)). What happens as T -> 0 and S ~ K?

options.text: Delta blows up (denominator -> 0, numerator bounded).  Hedging requires VERY frequent rebalancing -- practically impossible due to transaction costs.
Delta is constant in T -- no hedging concerns.

stages.explanation: Just like vanilla Gamma blows up ATM near expiry, the binary's delta itself blows up because the payoff becomes a step function. Replicating with a tight bull spread is one workaround: long 1/(2*epsilon) calls at K - epsilon, short 1/(2*epsilon) at K + epsilon, which approximates the digital as epsilon -> 0.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: Cash-or-nothing call price: c_B = exp(__[a]__) * N(__[b]__).  Hedging is hardest when S is __[c]__ K and T is __[d]__ (near 0 / large).
stages.explanation: c_B = exp(-rT) * N(d_2). Hardest to hedge: ATM, near expiry. Solution: tight bull spread approximation.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Compare a vanilla call's delta vs the binary's delta. (Vanilla: N(d_1). Binary: N'(d_2) / (S sigma sqrt(T)) -- essentially the vanilla's GAMMA times exp(-rT) / N'(d_1) ratio.)
Why is N(d_2) the RN prob and N(d_1) is NOT? (N(d_1) comes from a stock-numeraire measure, not the RN measure.)
Construct an asset-or-nothing call (pays S_T if S_T > K, else 0) -- price is S * N(d_1).
